searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation:Destroy() → () Cleans up the animation object and removes it from any associated threads.

searchDocs.title: Stop​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation:Stop() → () Stops the animation if it is currently playing.

searchDocs.title: Skip​
searchDocs.content: </> Animation:Skip() → () Immediately applies the animation's goal state to the target entity.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Destroy() → () Destroys the delay value object and cleans up its resources.

searchDocs.title: SetTime​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:SetTime(New: number) → () Sets the delay time.

searchDocs.title: For​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:For( Value: any, Time: number ) → () Sets a custom wait time for a value

searchDocs.title: Get​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Get() → any Returns the current value.

searchDocs.title: Set​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Set(newValue: any) → DelayValue Sets a new value.

searchDocs.title: Refresh​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Refresh() → () Triggers the changed signal.

searchDocs.title: SetSignalEnabled​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:SetSignalEnabled(Enabled: boolean) → ()

searchDocs.title: Sensitive​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:Sensitive() → DelayValue Marks the delay value as sensitive, meaning it won't send a signal if another update is in queue.

searchDocs.title: ResetTime​
searchDocs.content: </> DelayValue:ResetTime() → () Resets the delay time to the default time.

searchDocs.title: Play​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Play() → () Plays the animation.

searchDocs.title: Skip​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Skip() → () Skips the animation to the end state immediately.

searchDocs.title: Stop​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Stop() → () Stops the animation.

searchDocs.title: Pause​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Pause() → () Pauses the animation.

searchDocs.title: Resume​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Resume() → () Resumes the animation from where it was paused.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> LoadAnimation:Destroy() → () Destroys the animation object and cleans up resources.

searchDocs.title: SetId​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:SetId(Id: string) → () Sets a unique ID for the task. This ID can be used to identify the task later.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Connect() → () Connects a signal to the task. The task will be called when the signal is fired.

searchDocs.title: AddParameter​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:AddParameter() → () Cleans up the task and removes it from the Space. This will also disconnect any connections made to the task.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Destroy() → () Cleans up the task and removes it from the Space. This will also disconnect any connections made to the task.

searchDocs.title: Call​
searchDocs.content: </> Task:Call() → () Executes the task.

searchDocs.title: SetAttribute​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:SetAttribute( Attribute: string, Value: any ) → Create Similar to SetAttribute method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: GetAttribute​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:GetAttribute(Attribute: string) → Value Similar to the GetAttribute method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: GetAttributes​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:GetAttributes() → () Similar to the GetAttributes method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: AddTag​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:AddTag(Tag: string) → () Similar to the AddTag method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: RemoveTag​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:RemoveTag(Tag: string) → () Similar to the RemoveTag method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: HasTag​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:HasTag(Tag: string) → () Similar to the HasTag method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: GetTags​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:GetTags() → () Similar to the GetTags method for roblox instances

searchDocs.title: Clone​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:Clone() → () Clone the roblox object(Not faye Instance)

searchDocs.title: Compile​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:Compile() → () Compiles the properties of the instance.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> FayeInstance:Destroy() → () Destroys the instance.

searchDocs.title: Props​
searchDocs.content: When Creating or Configuring an item, the table is called a 'Props Table'  Thread:Create "Frame" { --this is a props table }   You can set properties of that entity in the Props Table and you can also use Faye Utilities to achieve reactivity or other stuff.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1,0,0) }

searchDocs.title: Functions​
searchDocs.content: Functions in props tables work like code blocks, everything they return will also be compiled, you can use them to do checks or add conditional components or properties. You can return components, Props tables, etc... if it doesn't error it will probably work.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; function() if true then return Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } else return {BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1,0,0)} end end }   You can also set properties to functions  { BackgroundColor3 = function() if true then return Color3.new(1,0,0) else return Color3.new(0,1,0) end end }   You can nest Props tables as much as you want and u can have as many functions as you want into them.

searchDocs.title: Signal​
searchDocs.content: Signal creates a reactive connection that executes a function when signals fire and compiles the returned props. Unlike regular event connections, Signal integrates with Faye's compilation system.  Thread:Create "TextButton" { Thread:Signal(button.MouseEnter, function(InnerThread, Entity) return { BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1, 0, 0) } end) }   The callback receives:  InnerThread: A thread for creating instancesEntity: The Roblox Instance being worked on...: Any additional arguments from the signal

searchDocs.title: Multiple signals​
searchDocs.content: You can listen to multiple signals at once by passing a table:  Thread:Create "TextButton" { Thread:Signal({button.MouseEnter, button.MouseLeave}, function(InnerThread, Entity) return { BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1, 1, 1) } end) }

searchDocs.title: Returning props​
searchDocs.content: You can return props in different ways:  -- Return a props table return {Text = "Clicked!", TextColor3 = Color3.new(1, 0, 0)} -- Return a specific property return "Text", "Clicked!" -- Return a new instance return InnerThread:Create "Frame" { ... }

searchDocs.title: SignalState​
searchDocs.content: SignalState is like Signal but cleans up the previous session before running the new one. This is useful when you're creating instances that should be replaced on each signal fire.  local Notifications = Thread:Value({}) Thread:Create "Frame" { Thread:SignalState(Notifications.Changed, function(InnerThread, Entity) -- Previous notifications are cleaned before this runs return InnerThread:Iterate(Notifications, function(i, notif, IterThread, Entity) return IterThread:Create "TextLabel" { Text = notif.message, Parent = Entity } end) end) }

searchDocs.title: Signal vs SignalState​
searchDocs.content: Feature	Signal	SignalStateCleans previous content	No	Yes Creates new InnerThread per fire	No	Yes Use for	Additive changes, property updates	Replacing content entirely

searchDocs.title: Using Signal outside props tables​
searchDocs.content: You can also use Signal standalone for general event handling:  local signal = Thread:Signal(workspace.ChildAdded, function(Thread, Entity, child) print("Child added:", child.Name) end) -- Later, to disconnect: signal:Destroy()   info Signal automatically cleans up connections when the parent Thread is destroyed, so you don't need to manually disconnect in most cases.

searchDocs.title: Call​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Call() → () Calls all tasks in the Space.

searchDocs.title: Add​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Add(Params: {[any]: any}) → () Adds/Creates a task to the Space. The task will be called when the Space is called.

searchDocs.title: Remove​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Remove(Task: Task) → () Removes a task from the Space. The task will no longer be called when the Space is called.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Connect( Connection: RBXScriptSignal , Id: string? ) → () Connects a signal to space, pass an id if u only want that connection to fire a specific task.

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Space:Destroy() → () Cleans up the Space and removes all tasks. This will also disconnect any connections made to the Space.

searchDocs.title: Basics​
searchDocs.content: Placing events in the props tables, will connect the listener  { Touched = function() end }   The first parameter of the events is always the Instance being worked on and following is all the other parameters  { Touched = function(Entity, PartTouched) end }   note Yes i am using an event reserved for parts, you can create anything with Faye.  Utilities help you implement special properties, reactivity, and animations to your Faye Instances. Utilities also work on Instances that are Configured using the Configure function.

searchDocs.title: MethodSignaled Utility​
searchDocs.content: The MethodSignaled utility allows you to listen to complex instance events like, GetAttributeChangedSignal, GetPropertyChangedSignal, etc...  { [Thread:MethodSignaled("GetPropertyChangedSignal","Size")] = function(Entity) local Size = Entity.AbsoluteSize print(Size) end, }   Its important to remember that complex roblox instance events don't return any parameters, so the only one that will be there is the one Faye automatically adds there which is the Entity in the first parameter position.  More MethodSignaled examples​  -- Listen to attribute changes Thread:Create "Frame" { [Thread:MethodSignaled("GetAttributeChangedSignal", "CustomData")] = function(Entity) local data = Entity:GetAttribute("CustomData") print("CustomData changed to:", data) end } -- Run callback on initialization too Thread:Create "Frame" { [Thread:MethodSignaled("GetPropertyChangedSignal", "AbsoluteSize", true)] = function(Entity) -- The third parameter `true` makes it run immediately on creation print("Size is:", Entity.AbsoluteSize) end }

searchDocs.title: Returning props from events​
searchDocs.content: Event handlers can return props to compile, just like Do and State:  Thread:Create "TextButton" { MouseEnter = function(Entity) return { BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1, 0, 0) } end, MouseLeave = function(Entity) return { BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1, 1, 1) } end }

searchDocs.title: Common events​
searchDocs.content: Event	Description	ParametersMouseEnter	Mouse enters the GUI element	Entity MouseLeave	Mouse leaves the GUI element	Entity MouseButton1Click	Left mouse click	Entity MouseButton2Click	Right mouse click	Entity Activated	Button activated (click/touch)	Entity, InputObject InputBegan	Input started on element	Entity, InputObject InputEnded	Input ended on element	Entity, InputObject Touched	Part touched (for BaseParts)	Entity, OtherPart  tip For more complex event handling with cleanup between fires, see Signal and SignalState.

searchDocs.title: DelayValue​
searchDocs.content: DelayValue wraps a Value and debounces its changes by a specified time. The Changed signal only fires after the delay has passed.  local SearchQuery = Thread:Value("") local DebouncedQuery = Thread:DelayValue(SearchQuery, 0.5) -- 500ms delay -- This won't spam API calls Thread:Reactive(function(Grab) local query = Grab(DebouncedQuery) if query ~= "" then searchAPI(query) end end)

searchDocs.title: Sensitive mode​
searchDocs.content: When sensitive mode is enabled, if a new update comes in before the delay finishes, the old update is skipped:  local Debounced = Thread:DelayValue(MyValue, 0.3):Sensitive()

searchDocs.title: Custom delays per value​
searchDocs.content: You can set different delays for specific values:  local Status = Thread:DelayValue(StatusValue, 1) Status:For("loading", 0) -- "loading" fires immediately Status:For("error", 2) -- "error" waits 2 seconds

searchDocs.title: Methods​
searchDocs.content: Debounced:Get() -- Get current value Debounced:Set(newValue) -- Set new value (with delay) Debounced:SetTime(0.5) -- Change delay time Debounced:ResetTime() -- Reset to default delay Debounced:Refresh() -- Force fire Changed signal Debounced:SetSignalEnabled(false) -- Disable Changed signal Debounced:Destroy() -- Clean up

searchDocs.title: DelayProperty​
searchDocs.content: DelayProperty lets you compile props after a delay. Useful for staged animations or delayed UI updates.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Size = UDim2.fromOffset(0, 0), -- After 1 second, animate to full size Thread:DelayProperty( {Size = Thread:Animation(UDim2.fromOffset(200, 200), Thread.Info(0.5))}, 1, -- delay in seconds {Size = UDim2.fromOffset(0, 0)} -- optional "From" state ) }   Parameters:  Props: The properties to compile after the delayTime: Delay in secondsFrom: Optional initial properties to set immediately

searchDocs.title: Space​
searchDocs.content: Space is a task batching system that helps manage multiple operations without excessive connections running at once.  local UpdateUI = Thread:Space(function(playerName, score) print(`Updating {playerName}: {score}`) end) -- Add tasks to the space local task1 = UpdateUI:Add({"Player1", 100}) local task2 = UpdateUI:Add({"Player2", 85}) -- Execute all tasks UpdateUI:Call()

searchDocs.title: Task methods​
searchDocs.content: local task = UpdateUI:Add({params}) task:SetId("unique-id") -- Set an ID for targeted calls task:Connect(signal) -- Auto-call when signal fires task:Call() -- Execute this task task:AddParameter(value) -- Add parameter task:Destroy() -- Remove from space

searchDocs.title: Space methods​
searchDocs.content: UpdateUI:Call() -- Execute all tasks UpdateUI:Call("specific-id") -- Execute only task with ID UpdateUI:Connect(signal) -- Connect signal to call all tasks UpdateUI:Connect(signal, "id") -- Connect signal to specific task UpdateUI:Remove(task) -- Remove a task UpdateUI:Destroy() -- Clean up entire space

searchDocs.title: YieldSafe​
searchDocs.content: YieldSafe lets you safely use yielding functions in props tables. Normally, yielding in compilation would block the UI.  Thread:Create "TextLabel" { Thread:YieldSafe(function(InnerThread, Entity) local data = fetchDataFromServer() -- This yields return { Text = data.message } end) }   With optional delay before execution:  Thread:YieldSafe(function(InnerThread, Entity) return {Text = "Delayed!"} end, 2) -- Wait 2 seconds before running

searchDocs.title: SetTo​
searchDocs.content: SetTo wraps a Value so that the property is set directly to the Value's current value without creating a reactive binding.  local InitialSize = Thread:Value(UDim2.fromOffset(100, 100)) Thread:Create "Frame" { -- Normally this would create a reactive binding Size = InitialSize, -- With SetTo, it just sets the value once (no reactivity) Position = Thread:SetTo(InitialPosition) }   This is useful when you want to use a Value's current state without subscribing to future changes.

searchDocs.title: Listener​
searchDocs.content: Listener connects a function to one or more events on an entity, passing the event name as the first argument.  Thread:Listener(textBox, {"FocusLost", "Focused"}, function(eventName, ...) if eventName == "Focused" then print("TextBox focused") elseif eventName == "FocusLost" then print("TextBox lost focus") end end)   This is useful when you want to handle multiple related events with a single function.

searchDocs.title: SpecialThread​
searchDocs.content: SpecialThread creates a dedicated thread for a function with custom lifecycle settings.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Thread:SpecialThread(function(InnerThread, Entity) -- This runs in its own thread return { BackgroundColor3 = Color3.new(1, 0, 0) } end, { Lifetime = 5, -- Auto-destroy after 5 seconds YieldSafe = true -- Allow yielding }) }

searchDocs.title: Parameters​
searchDocs.content: The second parameter can be:  A number: Lifetime in seconds before auto-cleanupA table with options: Lifetime: Seconds before auto-cleanupYieldSafe: Allow yielding in the function  -- Simple lifetime Thread:SpecialThread(func, 10) -- Cleanup after 10 seconds -- Full options Thread:SpecialThread(func, { Lifetime = 10, YieldSafe = true })

searchDocs.title: Do​
searchDocs.content: local Value = Thread:Value(33) Thread:Create "TextLabel" { Thread:Do(function(Grab, Thread, Entity) end) }   As you can see the first param is a Grab function, if you use it on any value it will return that Value's current value and will recall the Do utility everytime that value is updated. The second is the Thread that created the Do utility, for State its vital that you use it correctly but for Do since its just the Thread that created it, it isn't that important, and the Entity is the current Faye Instance in question just like in a function.  local Value = Thread:Value(33) Thread:Create "TextLabel" { Text = Thread:Do(function(Grab, Thread, Entity) local CurrentValue = Grab(Value) return `{CurrentValue} is the current number` end) }   Just like functions, you can create new Faye Instances with Do but it doesn't have a rince and repeat like system, so it will nnever delete the old stuff it created when it receives a new changed signal calling.

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: State uses a rince and repeat like system, so it will clean the last session before running the new one, so it solves the entire problem of the Do code block  local Value = Thread:Value(false) Thread:Create "TextLabel" { Thread:State(function(Grab, InnerThread, Entity) local CurrentValue = Grab(Value) if CurrentValue then return InnerThread:Create "Frame" { ... } else return { Text = "Unable to create frame" } end end) }   Everything created within the State utility must be created using the InnerThread provided on the 2nd parameter otherwise it won't clean previous sessions properly.

searchDocs.title: Reactive​
searchDocs.content: As you have probably already guessed, State, and Do only work in props tables, if u want a similar system to use outside of a props table you can use the Reactive utility.  local Value = Thread:Value(12) local ValueButAdd30 = Thread:Value() Thread:Reactive(function(Grab) local Current = Grab(Value) ValueButAdd30:Set(Current + 30) end)   The Reactive Utility, only has 1 parameter and its the Grab function.  info You can grab as many Values as you want and it will react to all of their changes, there's no rule of when u can grab them, it can be now or in the future

searchDocs.title: Faye is a Tool, Not a Runtime​
searchDocs.content: Unlike other Roblox UI frameworks (Roact, Fusion, etc.) that have their own runtime, virtual DOM, or reconciliation system, Faye is a utility toolkit that works directly with native Roblox instances.  What this means:  No virtual DOM or diffing overheadYou're always working with real Roblox instancesMix and match Faye with regular Roblox code freelyUse only the utilities you need - everything is modularNo "Faye way" of doing things - it enhances your workflow, doesn't replace it  Faye provides reactivity, lifecycle management, and animations as tools you can pick up and use, not as a framework that owns your UI.    Faye can be used in any way. You can also use the utility modules individually as you please. Most Faye Utilities have a Thread parameter you can use to insert your own Cleaner module(if used individually without a Faye Threader). You can also use roblox Instance values like how you would use Faye Values inside of Faye.

searchDocs.title: Object Creation and Configuration​
searchDocs.content: You can create objects using the Create function  Thread:Create("Frame") { ... }   The first element created using Thread:Create must have a Parent property inside its props table, its children will automatically have it as a parent in the roblox tree.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } }   The Configure function works on already created Faye Instances, or roblox Instances, it implements them into the Faye ecosystem letting you use Faye Utilities and other stuff on it.  Thread:Configure(PlayerGui.ScreenGui) { Thread:Create "Frame" { ... } }   local Frame = Thread:Create("Frame") { ... } Thread:Configure(Frame) { Position = ... }

searchDocs.title: Project Setup​
searchDocs.content: The ideal component based project setup looks like  local Faye = (...) return function(Parent: Instance) local Thread = Faye.new() ... return function() Thread:Destroy(); end) end)   The Thread allows you to keep track of what you create and allow you to clean everthing with one function. It is not needed, you can use Faye without a Thread as seen below  local Create = require(Faye.Create) return function(Parent: Instance) Create(...) end)   You can reuse some some Utilities, but a minority of them can't be reused, like the Create function, which creates a new Faye Instance, Faye Instances are unique due to the reactivity, so they can't be cloned and this is where the component system you might be familiar with comes in.  local EmptyFrame = function() return Create(...) end local Frame = EmptyFrame()   info If you will use Faye utilities without the Faye Thread, you can also get them with Faye[Utility name], you don't have to require the individual modules

searchDocs.title: Creating Threads​
searchDocs.content: local Faye = require(path.to.Faye) local Thread = Faye.new()

searchDocs.title: Extend​
searchDocs.content: Creates a sub-thread (child thread). Child threads are destroyed when their parent is destroyed.  local ChildThread = Thread:Extend() -- Create independent thread (won't be destroyed with parent) local IndependentThread = Thread:Extend(true)   When to use:  Creating scoped sections of your UIIsolating cleanup for specific featuresCreating threads for State/Iterate that need independent cleanup

searchDocs.title: NoAttachment (Independent Threads)​
searchDocs.content: When you pass true to Extend, the thread becomes independent:  local IndependentThread = Thread:Extend(true)   What this means:  The thread itself is NOT destroyed when the parent is destroyedYou must manually call :Destroy() on it when doneThe ParentThread reference is still kept for cleanup traversal  Important: Even though the thread is independent, cleanup functions inside it (from OnClean, Iterate, Signal, etc.) can still register with an ancestor thread. Faye walks up the ParentThread chain to find the nearest thread with cleanup responsibility. So:  local Parent = Faye.new() local Independent = Parent:Extend(true) Independent:Create "Frame" { Parent = someGui, OnClean = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- This cleanup function registers with Parent (via ParentThread traversal) -- It WILL run when Parent is destroyed print("Cleaning up") end } Parent:Destroy() -- The OnClean runs, but Independent thread itself still exists -- You'd need to call Independent:Destroy() separately   This design lets you have long-lived threads while still connecting specific cleanup behaviors to parent lifecycles.

searchDocs.title: Add / Remove​
searchDocs.content: Manually add or remove items from the thread's cleanup list.  -- Add items to be cleaned when thread is destroyed Thread:Add(connection) Thread:Add(instance) Thread:Add(function() print("cleanup") end) -- Add as priority (cleaned first) Thread:Add(importantConnection, true) -- Remove from cleanup list Thread:Remove(connection)   Supported types for cleanup:  Instances: :Destroy() is calledConnections: :Disconnect() is calledTables with Destroy/Clean: Method is calledFunctions: ExecutedThreads: task.cancel() is called

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: Shorthand for connecting signals and adding to cleanup.  -- Instead of: local conn = signal:Connect(handler) Thread:Add(conn, true) -- Just do: Thread:Connect(signal, handler)

searchDocs.title: Spawn / Delay​
searchDocs.content: Like task.spawn and task.delay but automatically tracked for cleanup.  -- Spawn a function Thread:Spawn(function() print("Running immediately") end) -- Delay a function Thread:Delay(2, function() print("Running after 2 seconds") end)   If the thread is destroyed before the delayed function runs, it's automatically cancelled.

searchDocs.title: Debris​
searchDocs.content: Like game.Debris:AddItem but tracked by the thread.  local part = Instance.new("Part") Thread:Debris(part, 5) -- Destroy after 5 seconds

searchDocs.title: Schedule​
searchDocs.content: Clean an item after a delay, or call a function after a delay.  -- Destroy instance after 3 seconds Thread:Schedule(3, myInstance) -- Disconnect after 2 seconds Thread:Schedule(2, myConnection) -- Call function after 1 second Thread:Schedule(1, function(arg1, arg2) print(arg1, arg2) end, "hello", "world") -- Immediate cleanup (Time = 0 or nil) Thread:Schedule(0, myInstance)

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: Destroys the thread and all its tracked resources.  Thread:Destroy()   This will:  Remove thread from parent (if any)Clean priority items firstClean all tracked resources in reverse orderClean child threads recursively  warning After calling :Destroy(), the thread is no longer usable. Don't try to add or create things with a destroyed thread.

searchDocs.title: Recommended Component Pattern​
searchDocs.content: local Faye = require(path.to.Faye) return function(Parent: Instance) local Thread = Faye.new() Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Parent, -- ... your UI } -- Return cleanup function return function() Thread:Destroy() end end   Usage:  local MyComponent = require(path.to.MyComponent) local cleanup = MyComponent(playerGui) -- Later, when done: cleanup()

searchDocs.title: Iterate​
searchDocs.content: Iterate creates a reactive iterator that refreshes the entire list when the source value changes. Use it when you want to re-render all items on any change.  local Items = Thread:Value({"Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"}) Thread:Create "Frame" { Thread:Iterate(Items, function(Index, Value, InnerThread, Entity) return InnerThread:Create "TextLabel" { Text = Value, LayoutOrder = Index, Parent = Entity } end) }   The callback function receives:  Index: The current key/index in the tableValue: The current value at that indexInnerThread: A thread for creating instances (use this for proper cleanup)Entity: The parent Roblox Instance  You can return properties to compile, a single instance, or a table of instances.

searchDocs.title: Iterating over numbers​
searchDocs.content: You can also iterate over a number to create N items:  local Count = Thread:Value(5) Thread:Create "Frame" { Thread:Iterate(Count, function(Index, Value, InnerThread, Entity) return InnerThread:Create "TextButton" { Text = `Button {Index}`, Parent = Entity } end) }   When Count changes, all buttons are cleaned and recreated.

searchDocs.title: AdvancedIterate​
searchDocs.content: AdvancedIterate is smarter - it only updates items that actually changed instead of refreshing everything. Each item gets its own dedicated thread for cleanup.  local Players = Thread:Value({ Player1 = {Name = "Alice", Score = 100}, Player2 = {Name = "Bob", Score = 85} }) Thread:Create "Frame" { Thread:AdvancedIterate(Players, function(Index, Value, InnerThread, Entity) return InnerThread:Create "TextLabel" { Text = `{Value.Name}: {Value.Score}`, Parent = Entity } end) }   When you add or remove items from the Value:  Players:Add("Player3", {Name = "Charlie", Score = 90}) -- Only creates Player3's UI Players:Remove("Player1") -- Only removes Player1's UI

searchDocs.title: When to use which?​
searchDocs.content: Use Case	RecommendedSmall lists that change entirely	Iterate Large lists with individual add/remove	AdvancedIterate Number-based iteration	Iterate Performance-critical lists	AdvancedIterate  info For AdvancedIterate to work properly with add/remove detection, you must use the Value's :Add() and :Remove() methods rather than replacing the entire table.

searchDocs.title: CloneTable​
searchDocs.content: CloneTable is a utility for cloning tables with optional transformation.

searchDocs.title: Basic usage​
searchDocs.content: local Original = {a = 1, b = 2, c = 3} local Copy = Thread:CloneTable(Original) -- Copy is now {a = 1, b = 2, c = 3}

searchDocs.title: With transformation function​
searchDocs.content: You can provide a function to transform each entry during cloning:  local Original = {a = 1, b = 2, c = 3} -- Double all values local Doubled = Thread:CloneTable(Original, function(Index, Value) return Index, Value * 2 end) -- Doubled is {a = 2, b = 4, c = 6}   The transform function can:  Return newIndex, newValue to change both key and valueReturn just newValue to keep the original keyReturn nil to exclude the entry from the clone  -- Filter and transform local Filtered = Thread:CloneTable(Original, function(Index, Value) if Value > 1 then return Index, Value * 10 end return nil -- Exclude entries where Value <= 1 end) -- Filtered is {b = 20, c = 30}

searchDocs.title: Special Faye Values​
searchDocs.content: Faye also offers some utilities that let you sync Values to properties and Attributes  local Value = Thread:InstanceAttributeSync(Instance,"SomeAttribute") -- Attribute sync local Value = Thread:InstancePropertySync(Instance,"Size") -- Property sync   You can use these special values like you would regular values, it has support for almost all functions if not all.

searchDocs.title: Useful Value functions​
searchDocs.content: You can use :Add , and :Remove to increment or decrement a Value's value, using + or - also works in their place.  local Value = Thread:Value(25) Value:Add(30) Value += 5 -- now the current value is 60 Value:Remove(40) Value -= 10 -- now its 10 Value = Value - 10 -- now its 0   This also works with table values  local tab = Thread:Value({ "Hello world" "Second" }) Tab -= "Second" -- removes second from the table

searchDocs.title: Refreshing a Value​
searchDocs.content: If you made no changes to a value, but want to fire the signal so that it triggers reactivity, you can force this by doing  Value:Refresh();

searchDocs.title: Listening to Value changes​
searchDocs.content: Every Value has a Changed signal you can connect to directly:  local Health = Thread:Value(100) Thread:Connect(Health.Changed, function(newValue) print("Health changed to:", newValue) end)

searchDocs.title: Table Values with keys​
searchDocs.content: When working with table Values, you can add and remove with specific keys:  local Players = Thread:Value({}) -- Add with key Players:Add("player1", {name = "Alice", score = 100}) -- Remove by key Players:Remove("player1")   This is especially useful with AdvancedIterate which can react to individual key changes.  There are more functions like this in the Api section.

searchDocs.title: OnClean​
searchDocs.content: OnClean is called before the instance is destroyed. It behaves differently depending on whether CleanDelay is also present:

searchDocs.title: OnClean without CleanDelay​
searchDocs.content: When used on its own, OnClean adds the instance to its InnerThread and waits for all animations in the InnerThread to finish before destroying. This is the recommended way to do cleanup animations.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = parent, OnClean = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- Entity is added to InnerThread automatically -- Faye waits for these animations to complete before destroying return { BackgroundTransparency = InnerThread:Animation(1, Thread.Info(0.3)) } end }

searchDocs.title: OnClean with CleanDelay​
searchDocs.content: When both are present, OnClean becomes a CleanFunction - it runs during cleanup but the instance destruction is controlled by the CleanDelay timer instead of waiting for animations.  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = parent, OnClean = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- This runs as a CleanFunction since CleanDelay is present -- Destruction timing is controlled by CleanDelay, not animations return { BackgroundTransparency = InnerThread:Animation(1, Thread.Info(0.5)) } end, CleanDelay = 0.5 }   The callback receives:  InnerThread: A dedicated thread for cleanup operationsEntity: The Roblox Instance being cleaned

searchDocs.title: CleanDelay​
searchDocs.content: CleanDelay delays the destruction of an instance by a specified time (in seconds).  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = parent, CleanDelay = 2 -- Wait 2 seconds before destroying }

searchDocs.title: Dynamic delay​
searchDocs.content: You can also pass a function to calculate the delay:  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = parent, CleanDelay = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- Calculate delay based on some condition return Entity.AbsoluteSize.X > 100 and 1 or 0.5 end }

searchDocs.title: CleanFunction​
searchDocs.content: CleanFunction runs during cleanup but does not control when the instance is destroyed. Unlike OnClean (without CleanDelay), it won't wait for animations to finish.  Thread:Create "Frame" { CleanFunction = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- Runs during cleanup, but doesn't delay destruction print("Cleaning up") end }   info When you use OnClean together with CleanDelay, the OnClean is internally treated as a CleanFunction. Use OnClean alone if you want Faye to automatically wait for animations to finish.

searchDocs.title: InnerThread​
searchDocs.content: When you use OnClean or CleanDelay, Faye automatically creates an InnerThread for the instance. This thread:  Is a child of the main threadGets destroyed after the instance cleanup completesCan be used to track cleanup-specific resources  Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = parent, OnClean = function(InnerThread, Entity) -- InnerThread is automatically created -- Use it for cleanup animations/operations InnerThread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Entity, -- This child will also be cleaned properly } end }

searchDocs.title: Cleanup Order​
searchDocs.content: When a thread is destroyed:  Priority items are cleaned first (connections added with Thread:Add(item, true))Regular items are cleaned in reverse order (last added = first cleaned)Child threads are recursively cleanedInstance cleanup runs (CleanFunction if present, then OnClean if present)For OnClean without CleanDelay: waits for all InnerThread animations to finish, then destroysFor CleanDelay: waits the delay duration, then destroys

searchDocs.title: Do use OnClean for:​
searchDocs.content: Fade out animationsLogging/analyticsNotifying other systemsCleaning external resources

searchDocs.title: Don't use OnClean for:​
searchDocs.content: Long-running operations (blocks cleanup)Creating new permanent UI (it will be destroyed)Heavy computations

searchDocs.title: Example: Notification with auto-dismiss​
searchDocs.content: local function Notification(message, duration) local Thread = Faye.new() Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = notificationContainer, Thread:Create "TextLabel" { Text = message }, OnClean = function(InnerThread, Entity) return { Position = InnerThread:Animation( UDim2.new(1, 0, 0, 0), Thread.Info(0.3) ) } end, CleanDelay = 0.3 } -- Auto-destroy after duration Thread:Delay(duration, function() Thread:Destroy() end) return Thread end

searchDocs.title: Reactive Animations​
searchDocs.content: Before creating reactive animations, you need to know about the Info tables and the Modification tables

searchDocs.title: Info​
searchDocs.content: local Info = require(Faye.Info) local SpringInfo = require(Faye.SpringInfo) local AnimInfo = Info( -- or Thread.Info or Faye.Info Time: number?, EasingStyle: Enum.EasingStyle?, EasingDirection: Enum.EasingDirection?, RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? ) local AnimSpringInfo = SpringInfo( -- or Thread.SpringInfo or Faye.Info Time: number?, Frequency: number?, -- frequency Damping: number?, --loss of energy RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? )   These are similar to to TweenService's TweenInfo, with an Addition of SpringInfo, If you want to Use Springs instead of the plain roblox easings you substitute the Info with a SpringInfo when creating an animation, its that easy.  note If you leave any empty fields, it will automatically fill it with default values. You can also use a table for the info, these 'Info' and 'SpringInfo' functions just help you structure it with suggestions

searchDocs.title: Modification Tables​
searchDocs.content: local Modifications = { FirstGoal: any; -- The goal for the first play FirstDelayTime: number?; -- the DelayTime for the first play FirstInfo: (Info | SpringInfo)?; -- the Info for the first play From: any; -- makes it so the animation starts from this once AlwaysFrom: any; --makes it so the animation always start from this }   These are self explanatory and allow you to have special modifications for your animations

searchDocs.title: Animation Creation​
searchDocs.content: The first parameter is the goal, the second the Info, and the third the modification table, the only mandatory parameter is the goal, the rest can be left empty.  Thread:Create "Frame" { BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation(Color3.new(1,1,0),InfoTable); -- tweens smoothly to the color }   Above we have a tween that starts from the current Frame Color to the Goal  --test this code yourself in studio local Faye = require(...) -- set this to the right path return function(Target) local Thread = Faye.new() Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation( Color3.new(1,1,0) , Thread.Info(.5) , {From = Color3.new(1,0,0)} ); -- Starts from red } return function() Thread:Destroy() end end     Rather than our goal, we can substitute it with a Faye Value, and everytime we update it, our Frame will react to its change  local Goal = Thread:Value(Color3.new(1,1,0)) Thread:Create "Frame" { Parent = Target; BackgroundColor3 = Thread:Animation( Goal , Thread.Info(.5) , {From = Color3.new(1,0,0)} ); -- Starts from red MouseEnter = function() Goal:Set(Color3.new(0,0,1)) end, MouseLeave = function() Goal:Reset() end }     note When using Faye values as Goal, the animation won't tween to the goal for the first iteration, it will always set the Instance's property to the Goal's initial value first, so if you want it to tween it to, you must specify the From in the Animation modifications table, then it will tween to the Goal rather than setting the object's property on initialization.

searchDocs.title: Modifying already created Animations​
searchDocs.content: Lets say you cloned an animation using table.clone and want to add special modifications to it, you can use the ModifyAnimation utility  local Anim = Thread:Animation(Color3.new(1,0,0),Info) local AnimClone = table.clone(Anim) Thread:ModifyAnimation(AnimClone, ModificationsTable)   You can also skip the Cloning part, because Faye has a built in function to do all of this in one go  local Anim = Thread:Animation(Color3.new(1,0,0),Info) local AnimClone = Thread:CloneAnimation(Anim,ModificationsTable) Thread:Create "Frame" { BackgroundColor3 = AnimClone }

searchDocs.title: Quick animations​
searchDocs.content: If you just want to play animations and you don't want to setup reactivity, you can use the Faye's LoadAnimation utility, use it within a thread for the Thread to manage cleaning, but its not necessary, You can use it in any script in ur game.  Thread:LoadAnimation( Entity , {Size = UDim2.fromOffset(200,200)} , Thread.Info(.2) ):Play()   You can tween multiple properties in one object at the same time just like regular roblox tweening, the Info parameter is optional, but the Object and Goal table must be specified.

searchDocs.title: LoadAnimation Methods​
searchDocs.content: local anim = Thread:LoadAnimation(entity, {Size = UDim2.fromOffset(200, 200)}, Thread.Info(0.5)) anim:Play() -- Start the animation anim:Pause() -- Pause at current position anim:Resume() -- Resume from paused position anim:Stop() -- Stop and reset anim:Skip() -- Jump to end state immediately anim:Destroy() -- Clean up the animation object

searchDocs.title: Example: Button hover animation​
searchDocs.content: local button = Thread:Create "TextButton" { Parent = parent, Size = UDim2.fromOffset(100, 50), } local hoverAnim = Thread:LoadAnimation(button.Instance, { Size = UDim2.fromOffset(110, 55) }, Thread.Info(0.2, Enum.EasingStyle.Quad)) button.Instance.MouseEnter:Connect(function() hoverAnim:Play() end) button.Instance.MouseLeave:Connect(function() hoverAnim:Stop() end)   info You can reuse 1 animation utility on multiple objects

searchDocs.title: CloneAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.CloneAnimation( Animaiton: {any:any}, Modifications: AnimationModifications ) → {any:any}? Clones an animation table and applies any provided modifications.

searchDocs.title: CloneTable​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.CloneTable( Table: {any:any}, cloneFunction: ( Index: any, Value: any ) → ( any?, any? )? ) → {any:any}? Clones a table. If a cloneFunction is provided, it will be used to determine how to clone each entry.

searchDocs.title: Info​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.Info( Time: number?, EasingStyle: Enum.EasingStyle?, EasingDirection: Enum.EasingDirection?, RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? ) → Info Creates a new Info object with the given parameters.

searchDocs.title: SpringInfo​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread.SpringInfo( Time: number?, Frequency: number?, Damping: number?, RepeatCount: number?, Reverse: boolean?, DelayTime: number? ) → SpringInfo Creates a SpringInfo object that can be used in animations to define spring-like behavior.

searchDocs.title: AdvancedIterate​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:AdvancedIterate( Tab: any, Func: ( Index: any, Value: any, InnerThread: Types.Thread?, Entity: Instance? ) → () ) → () Creates a reactive iterator that reacts to the table's individually changes and updates them accordingly.

searchDocs.title: Animation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Animation( Goal: any,-- The target value or state for the animation Info: Types.Info | Types.SpringInfo?,-- (Optional) The animation info Modifications: Types.AnimationModifications?-- (Optional) Modifications to apply to the animation ) → Animation Creates an animation object with the specified goal, info, and optional modifications.

searchDocs.title: Configure​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Configure(Entity: any) → function Allows configuration on an existing physical or none physical component. Inherits method from Create.

searchDocs.title: Create​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Create(ClassName: string) → (props: PropertyTypes.Property) → Create Creates a new instance object.

searchDocs.title: DelayProperty​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:DelayProperty( Props: Types.Properties, Time: number, From: any ) → DelayProperty Creates a new delay object that will compile the given properties after [Time] time.

searchDocs.title: DelayValue​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:DelayValue( Value: Types.Value, Time: number ) → DelayValue Creates a new delay value object.

searchDocs.title: Do​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Do(func: ( Grab: any, InnerThread: Thread, Entity: Instance? ) → ) → Do Creates a Do object that can be used in Faye's threading system to execute functions.

searchDocs.title: Extend​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Extend(NoLink: boolean) → Thread Creates a sub-thread. Leave the NoLink to nil and the thread will be linked to the parent thread(will delete with parent), set NoLink to true for the thread to be independent.

searchDocs.title: Spawn​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Spawn() → () Similar to Task.spawn, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Delay​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Delay() → () Similar to Task.Delay, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Debris​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Debris() → () Similar to game.debris:AddItem, not to be used within a compileable table.

searchDocs.title: Clean​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Clean() → () This method cleans everything added to the thread, and the thread itself. After this method gets called the thread is no longer useable.

searchDocs.title: Connect​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Connect( Connection: RBXScriptSignal , Function:: () → () ) → () Signal connection

searchDocs.title: Add​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Add(Entity: any) → () The entity to add to the thread

searchDocs.title: Remove​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Remove(Entity: any) → () The entity to remove from the thread, meaning when the thread is cleaned it won't be cleaned with it.

searchDocs.title: Schedule​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Schedule( Time: number?, Item: any, ...: any ) → item Cleans an item individually based off the time. can also be used to call functions after a time has passed.

searchDocs.title: InstanceAttributeSync​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:InstanceAttributeSync( Entity: Instance , Property: string ) → InstanceAttributeSync Creates a Value object that syncs with a specific attribute of an Instance.

searchDocs.title: InstancePropertySync​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:InstancePropertySync( Entity: Instance , Property: string ) → InstancePropertySync Creates a Value object that syncs with a specific property of an Instance.

searchDocs.title: Iterate​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Iterate( Tab: any, Func: ( Index: any, Value: any, InnerThread: Thread?, Entity: Instance? ) → () ) → Iterate Creates a reactive iterator, when the value changes it refreshes the entire thing.

searchDocs.title: Listener​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Listener( Entity: any, Event(s): any, Function: function ) → () Connects the given function to the specified event(s) on the entity.

searchDocs.title: LoadAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:LoadAnimation( Entity: Instance ,-- The instance to animate Goal: Types.Properties,-- The goal properties to animate to Info: Types.Info?-- (Optional) The animation info ) → () return LoadAnimation -- The playable animation object

searchDocs.title: MethodSignaled​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:MethodSignaled( Method: string, Index: string, RunOnInitialization: boolean? ) → () Listener for methods, example: 'GetPropertyChangedSignal', 'GetAttributeChangedSignal', etc.

searchDocs.title: ModifyAnimation​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:ModifyAnimation( Animaiton: {[any]: any}, Modifications: Types.AnimationModifications ) → ModifyAnimation Lets you add modifications to an existing animation object, doesn't work with animation objects created using LoadAnimation.

searchDocs.title: Reactive​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Reactive(func: (Grab: (Value: Instance | Types.Value) → any) → ()) → Reactive Creates a reactive function that re-runs whenever any of the grabbed values change.

searchDocs.title: SetTo​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SetTo(Value: any) → SetTo Wraps a value into a SetTo value object that can be used in props tables.

searchDocs.title: Signal​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Signal( Connections: RBXScriptSignal | {RBXScriptSignal },-- The signal or list of signals to connect to func: (...any) → ()?-- The function to execute when the signal is ) → Signal Creates a signal object that listens to the specified connections and executes the provided function in a reactive manner.

searchDocs.title: SignalState​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SignalState( Connections: RBXScriptSignal | {RBXScriptSignal }, func: (...any) → ()? ) → SignalState Creates a reactive signal that runs the provided function whenever any of the provided connections fire, this one deletes the old components before loading the new returned returned ones.

searchDocs.title: Space​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Space(func: function) → () Creates a new Space object. The space will call the function when the space is called.

searchDocs.title: SpecialThread​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:SpecialThread( func: ( Thread: Types.Thread, Entity: Instance ) → (), Params: (Types.SpecialThreadParams | number)? ) → SpecialThread Creates a special thread object that can be used in Faye's threading system with custom settings.

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:State(func: ( Grab: any, InnerThread: Thread, Entity: Instance? ) → ()) → State Creates a State object that can be used in Faye's reactive system, it deletes the old components before loading the new ones.

searchDocs.title: Value​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:Value(Initial: any) → Value Creates a Value object that can be used in Faye's reactive system.

searchDocs.title: YieldSafe​
searchDocs.content: </> Thread:YieldSafe( func: () → (), DelayTime: number? ) → YieldSafe Creates a YieldSafe object that can be used to run functions with yields safely.
